reasoning: The base rate for MEP resignations suggests a moderate probability of at least one resignation over a three-week window. While a previous parliamentary term (2004–2009) saw 120 resignations over five years (a rate that would yield roughly a 75% chance in 21 days), more recent terms have seen somewhat fewer routine mid-term replacements, bringing the base rate down to roughly 50-60%. The active 'Huawei scandal' introduces political pressure, but MEPs under investigation (such as those in Qatargate) typically do not resign to avoid losing their parliamentary immunity and salary; the immunity waiver process takes months. Without a specific documented resignation announcement pending, relying on the base rate for 720 MEPs over a 21-day period leads to an estimate of around 58%.
